In part 1 of this article, we talked about how ancient Express.js feels compared to modern, fast alternatives like Fastify, NestJS, or Hono. We’ve all felt that urge to throw away old, legacy boilerplate and rewrite our backends using new tools that actually respect TypeScript.
So why does Express still handle the vast majority of production web traffic across the entire Node ecosystem?
1. The Simplicity of (req, res, next)
Express’s true superpower isn’t its performance, its CLI, or its feature set. It’s the sheer simplicity of its middleware pattern.
app.use((req, res, next) => {
if (req.headers['x-api-key']) {
next();
} else {
res.status(401).send('Unauthorized');
}
});That’s it. That simple, three-argument function signature (req, res, next) is the entire model of Express.
Because that pattern was so damn simple to grasp, millions of developers understood it instantly. Over the last decade, almost every library author on Earth built their tools for Express first:

• Need authentication? passport

• Need security headers? helmet

• Need CORS handling, rate limiting, or file uploads? cors, express-rate-limit, multer
When you switch frameworks, you don’t just learn a new routing syntax — you often give up a decade’s worth of battle-tested community middleware that handles every edge case imaginable.
2. The Bandwagon
Yes, Fastify can log 70,000 HTTP requests per second in a test case while Express might limit at 15,000. On a YouTube thumbnail or Twitter bar chart, Fastify looks 4x better.

But what happens the moment you add a real database query to both frameworks?
Framework Overhead: [Express: 0.5ms] vs [Fastify: 0.1ms]
Database Query: [PostgreSQL Disk Search: 45.0ms]
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Total Response Time: Express = 45.5ms | Fastify = 45.1msThe second your route handler makes an asynchronous call to PostgreSQL, MongoDB, or an external payment gateway, that 0.4ms framework advantage completely dissapears.
Your end users will never feel the difference between a 45.1ms response and a 45.5ms response. Express’s “slow speed” is literally invisible in 95% of real-world business applications.
3. The Cost of Switching Up
Imagine walking into team planning and convincing a team of eight developers to migrate a core service from Express to NestJS.
Suddenly, everyone on the team has to master Nest’s specific abstractions: Modules, Controllers, Providers, Injectables, and TypeScript Decorators. Pull request reviews slow down, bugs sneak into production during the learning curve, and internal utility libraries have to be completely rewritten.
Express might be boring, but boring means every developer on Earth knows how to read it from day one. An intern, a junior developer, or an experienced backend engineer can open an Express route file and immediately understand what it does within five minutes.
How to Choose Without Feeling Guilty
It is completely normal to look at Express and feel a bit tired of seeing it everywhere. As developers, we naturally want to work with tools that feel fast, modern, and elegant.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Lesson Learned
Learning new frameworks like Fastify, Hono, or NestJS is fantastic. They teach you modern design patterns, make you to write cleaner code, and make side projects fun again.
But don’t look down on Express just because it’s older than your favorite backend library. Express survived for over a decade because it got the single most important thing right: it stayed out of our way. In backend engineering, predictable stability and zero surprises always win in the end.
